## Stationary gravity and the operator $\hat M_g$

The gravity chapter established the stationary gravity route at the classical and local-readout level. A source builds a stationary field; the local observer then reads clocking and transport through that field. The two local quantities that matter most here are the contextual fermic scale $p_{f,c}$ and the transport momentum $\Pi_i$.

The quantum operator should not ignore that structure. If the flat operator is

$$
\hat M=\beta p_f+\boldsymbol\alpha\cdot\hat{\mathbf p},
$$

then the stationary gravity version should deform exactly the two slots that the gravity chapter says gravity deforms:

$$
p_f\longrightarrow p_{f,c}(\mathbf x),
\qquad
\hat{\mathbf p}\longrightarrow\hat{\boldsymbol\Pi}.
$$

The scalar replacement is

$$
p_{f,c}(\mathbf x)=p_f\sqrt{-g_{00}(\mathbf x)}.
$$

This is the quantum-side use of the same local clocking rule introduced in the gravity chapter. Static gravity dresses the intrinsic fermic scale before the particle's directed motion is read.

The directional replacement is the mechanical momentum operator

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol\Pi}=\hat{\mathbf p}-\mathbf A_g(\hat{\mathbf x}),
$$

where $\mathbf A_g$ is the stationary shift potential written in momentum units in the simplified convention used here. In a more explicit spatial-frame notation, the contraction with the Dirac matrices should be read locally as $\alpha^a e_a{}^i\hat\Pi_i$, where $e_a{}^i$ is a spatial frame for the stationary background. In a local orthonormal frame this reduces to the compact dot product used below.

The gravity-dressed core momentum operator is therefore

$$
\boxed{
\hat M_g=\beta p_{f,c}(\hat{\mathbf x})
+\boldsymbol\alpha\cdot\hat{\boldsymbol\Pi}
}.
$$

This is not a separate gravitational theory pasted onto the quantum construction. It is the operator version of the same stationary readout rule already used classically: scalar gravity dresses the fermic scale, and shift structure modifies directed transport.

### Compatibility with the gravity bookkeeping

The essential compatibility check is local. Ignore, for the moment, gradients and operator-ordering effects, and read $\hat M_g$ through its principal symbol. The Dirac algebra then gives the same quadratic package as in the flat case, but with the stationary gravity replacements inserted:

$$
\sigma(\hat M_g)^2
=p_{f,c}^2+\gamma^{ij}\Pi_i\Pi_j.
$$

The square root of that symbol is exactly the local packaged magnitude from the gravity chapter,

$$
M_{g,loc}=\sqrt{p_{f,c}^2+\gamma^{ij}\Pi_i\Pi_j}.
$$

This is the main compatibility result. The operator is new only in quantum packaging; its leading local scalar reading is the same stationary gravity quantity already developed classically. The route is therefore explicit:

$$
\hat M_g
\longrightarrow
\sigma(\hat M_g)
\longrightarrow
M_{g,loc}.
$$

In the flat limit,

$$
p_{f,c}\to p_f,
\qquad
\mathbf A_g\to0,
\qquad
\gamma^{ij}\to\delta^{ij},
$$

so $\hat M_g\to\hat M$ and $M_{g,loc}\to M$.

The bridge is summarized in @fig-qm-operator-bridge. The flat operator packages the fermic scale and directed momentum operator into $\hat M$. Stationary gravity then dresses the two corresponding slots, $p_f\to p_{f,c}$ and $\hat{\mathbf p}\to\hat{\boldsymbol\Pi}$, giving $\hat M_g$. The local readout recovers $M_{g,loc}$, while the same scalar dressing previews the phase and barrier-exponent sensitivity returned to later.

![The M1 quantum operator bridge from the flat core momentum operator to its stationary gravity-dressed form. The flat operator $\hat M$ packages $p_f$ and $\hat{\mathbf p}$; stationary gravity dresses those slots into $p_{f,c}$ and $\hat{\boldsymbol\Pi}$, giving $\hat M_g$. The local/principal scalar reading recovers $M_{g,loc}$, while strong scalar dressing previews phase and barrier-exponent sensitivity.](../../../../figures/build/quantum-mechanics/qm-operator-bridge.png){#fig-qm-operator-bridge fig-align="center" width="86%"}

The omitted terms are also meaningful. When $p_{f,c}(\mathbf x)$ or $\mathbf A_g(\mathbf x)$ varies across the wavefunction, the operator pieces no longer commute. Squaring $\hat M_g$ then produces gradient, circulation, and spin-coupled terms. Those terms are not a sign that the operator is incompatible with gravity. They are the quantum corrections one should expect when a phase-bearing state samples a structured stationary background.

Before those corrections are used as novelty claims, the correspondence limits must be fixed. In the slow-motion and slowly varying limit, the extra terms reduce to the ordinary local readout. In more sensitive quantum regimes, the same structure becomes the channel through which gravity can affect phase evolution, local envelope dynamics, spin transport, interference, and tunneling exponents. The strong-gravity consequence is sharper once the standard limits are in place.
